overview.introduction: Linux gives you precise control over running processes and network connections. Understanding how to inspect, manage, and troubleshoot them is essential when diagnosing why an application won't start or a port is blocked.
overview.why_it_matters: In production, services crash, ports conflict, and network connections time out. These commands are the first tools you reach for when debugging a failing server, a hung process, or a connectivity issue.
overview.industry_usage: Used by Site Reliability Engineers (SREs), DevOps engineers, and backend developers to debug running applications, manage system resources, investigate port conflicts, and monitor network traffic.
overview.what_you_will_build: You will identify running processes, kill hung applications, inspect open network ports, and diagnose connectivity — simulating real production debugging scenarios.
overview.prerequisites: Linux Command Line Basics and Bash Scripting — you should know how to navigate, create files, and run scripts.
learning_outcomes: List running processes using ps and top.
Find and kill a process by name or PID.
Check which ports are open and listening using ss and netstat.
Diagnose network connectivity with ping and curl.
Run processes in the background and manage them with fg/bg.

key_concepts.title: Understanding Linux Processes
key_concepts.definition: A process is a running instance of a program. Every command you run, every application that starts — each becomes a process with a unique Process ID (PID).
why_important: Identify what is consuming CPU or memory on an overloaded server.
Find and stop hung processes that are blocking resources.
Understand parent-child relationships — killing a parent kills its children.
Monitor daemon processes (web servers, databases) to confirm they are running.
key_concepts.explanation:
Every process has:
- **PID**: Unique Process ID number
- **PPID**: Parent Process ID (who started it)
- **User**: Which user account owns it
- **State**: Running, Sleeping, Stopped, Zombie
- **CPU/Memory**: Resource usage

Process hierarchy example:
```
PID 1 (systemd/init)
└── PID 1234 (nginx)
    └── PID 1235 (nginx worker)
    └── PID 1236 (nginx worker)
```

Common process states:

| State | Code | Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Running | R | Actively executing on CPU |
| Sleeping | S | Waiting for I/O or event |
| Stopped | T | Paused (Ctrl+Z) |
| Zombie | Z | Exited but parent hasn't read exit code |

commands.name: ps aux
commands.purpose: Shows a snapshot of all currently running processes with details.
commands.parameters: a: all users, u: user-oriented format, x: include processes without terminal.
commands.expected_output:
www-data  1234  0.0  0.1  12345  4567 ?  S  10:30  0:00 nginx: master process
www-data  1235  0.0  0.0   9876  2345 ?  S  10:30  0:00 nginx: worker process
commands.output_explanation: Shows nginx master and worker processes: user (www-data), PID, CPU%, memory%, and command.
commands.when_to_use: Use to check if a service is running or to find the PID of a specific process.
commands.real_world_use: Run ps aux | grep java to confirm a Java application server is running and see its PID.

commands.name: top
commands.purpose: Shows real-time, continuously updated process resource usage.
commands.parameters: Press q to quit. Press k to kill a process by PID. Press M to sort by memory.
commands.expected_output:
top - 10:35:01 up 5 days, 2 users, load average: 0.12, 0.08, 0.05
PID   USER  PR NI  VIRT RES SHR S %CPU %MEM COMMAND
1234  root  20  0 12456 456  123 S  0.3  0.1  nginx
commands.output_explanation: Shows live CPU/memory usage per process. Load average: system workload over 1, 5, 15 minutes.
commands.when_to_use: Use during performance issues to find which process is consuming the most resources.
commands.real_world_use: An SRE opens top during a high CPU alert to identify which process is spiking and causing the issue.

key_concepts.pro_tip: Use `htop` if available — it's a colorized, interactive version of top with much better UX.
key_concepts.best_practices: Check ps aux before assuming a service has failed — it may be running under a different name or user.
key_concepts.common_mistakes: Grepping ps output and seeing your grep command itself in results: `ps aux | grep nginx` might show the grep process too. Filter with `grep -v grep`.
key_concepts.real_world_example: An SRE gets paged that the web app is down. They run ps aux | grep nginx to confirm nginx is running, then check the logs for errors.
key_concepts.remember: PID 1 is always the init system (systemd on modern Linux). Everything else is a child or descendant of PID 1.
key_concepts.summary: ps aux gives a full snapshot of processes. top shows live resource usage. Both are essential for diagnosing server health.

key_concepts.title: Killing Processes
key_concepts.definition: The kill command sends a signal to a process. Signals tell a process to stop, restart, reload config, or perform other actions.
why_important: Stop a hung or zombie process that is consuming resources.
Gracefully reload application config without full restart.
Clean up runaway processes before they exhaust memory or CPU.
Stop services before performing maintenance or upgrades.
key_concepts.explanation:
Linux uses signals to communicate with processes:

| Signal | Number | Name | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| SIGTERM | 15 | Terminate | Ask process to stop gracefully |
| SIGKILL | 9 | Kill | Force stop immediately (cannot be caught) |
| SIGHUP | 1 | Hangup | Reload configuration |
| SIGINT | 2 | Interrupt | Same as Ctrl+C |

**Golden rule**: Always try SIGTERM (15) first. Only use SIGKILL (9) if the process won't respond.

Why does this matter?
- SIGTERM allows the app to save state, close connections, flush buffers.
- SIGKILL immediately kills the process — data may be lost or connections left open.

commands.name: kill PID
commands.purpose: Sends SIGTERM (graceful stop request) to a process by PID.
commands.parameters: PID: the numeric process ID. Get it from ps aux or pgrep.
commands.expected_output: (No output if successful — process stops gracefully)
commands.output_explanation: SIGTERM sent to PID 1234. The process can finish current work and clean up before exiting.
commands.when_to_use: Always try this first before SIGKILL.
commands.real_world_use: Gracefully stop a web server before a rolling deployment update.

commands.name: kill -9 PID
commands.purpose: Sends SIGKILL — immediately and forcefully terminates a process.
commands.parameters: -9: SIGKILL signal. PID: process to terminate.
commands.output_explanation: The OS kills PID 1234 immediately without giving it a chance to clean up.
commands.when_to_use: Only when kill PID fails and the process is truly stuck.
commands.real_world_use: A Jenkins build agent is stuck for 2 hours. After kill fails, kill -9 forces it to terminate.

commands.name: pkill
commands.purpose: Kills processes by name instead of PID.
commands.parameters: process_name: name or pattern to match against running processes.
commands.output_explanation: All processes matching the name 'nginx' receive SIGTERM.
commands.when_to_use: Use when you know the process name but don't want to look up the PID first.
commands.real_world_use: pkill -9 java to forcefully stop all Java processes during an emergency restart.

key_concepts.pro_tip: Use `pgrep -l processname` to list matching process names and PIDs before killing — confirm you are killing the right process.
key_concepts.best_practices: Never use kill -9 as your first action. Always try SIGTERM first and give the process 5-10 seconds to stop.
key_concepts.common_mistakes: Killing a parent process without knowing it will also kill all its children — verify the process tree with pstree or ps first.
key_concepts.real_world_example: During a Kubernetes node drain, kubelet sends SIGTERM to pods and waits 30 seconds (grace period) before SIGKILL. This is the same pattern applied at container scale.
key_concepts.remember: Signal 15 (SIGTERM) = 'please stop'. Signal 9 (SIGKILL) = 'stop now, no choice'. Always try 15 first.
key_concepts.summary: kill sends SIGTERM for graceful stop. kill -9 forces immediate termination. pkill matches by name.

key_concepts.title: Network Ports and Connections
key_concepts.definition: Network ports are numbered channels (0-65535) through which applications communicate. Checking which ports are open tells you which services are running and accessible.
why_important: Confirm a web server is listening on port 80/443 before testing in a browser.
Identify port conflicts when two services try to bind to the same port.
Audit open ports for security — unexpected open ports indicate misconfiguration.
Diagnose firewall rules blocking expected service connections.
key_concepts.explanation:
Common well-known ports:

| Port | Service |
|:---|:---|
| 22 | SSH |
| 80 | HTTP |
| 443 | HTTPS |
| 3306 | MySQL |
| 5432 | PostgreSQL |
| 6379 | Redis |
| 8080 | Common app port |
| 27017 | MongoDB |

Connection states:

| State | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| LISTEN | Port is open, waiting for connections |
| ESTABLISHED | Active connection |
| TIME_WAIT | Connection closing (waiting for late packets) |
| CLOSE_WAIT | Remote end closed — local app should close too |

commands.name: ss -lntp
commands.purpose: Lists all TCP ports that are listening for incoming connections.
commands.parameters: -l: listening sockets, -n: show numeric ports (no name resolution), -t: TCP, -p: show process name.
commands.expected_output:
State  Recv-Q Send-Q  Local Address:Port  Peer Address:Port  Process
LISTEN 0      128     0.0.0.0:80          0.0.0.0:*          users:(("nginx",pid=1234,fd=6))
LISTEN 0      128     0.0.0.0:22          0.0.0.0:*          users:(("sshd",pid=890,fd=3))
commands.output_explanation: nginx is listening on port 80 (HTTP), sshd on port 22 (SSH). Both are ready to accept connections.
commands.when_to_use: Use immediately when an app isn't responding to verify it's actually listening on the expected port.
commands.real_world_use: After deploying a Node.js app, run ss -lntp to confirm it's bound to port 3000 before testing.

commands.name: curl
commands.purpose: Makes HTTP requests from the command line to test web endpoints.
commands.parameters: -I: headers only, -s: silent, -o: output to file, -v: verbose.
commands.expected_output:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: nginx/1.18.0
Content-Type: text/html
commands.output_explanation: HTTP 200 OK means the web server responded successfully. Headers show server type and content type.
commands.when_to_use: Test if a web server or API endpoint is responding correctly from the command line.
commands.real_world_use: CI health check: curl -f http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1 — fails the build if the app doesn't respond.

key_concepts.pro_tip: Use `lsof -i :PORT` to see exactly which process is using a specific port: `lsof -i :8080`
key_concepts.best_practices: Always check ss -lntp after deploying an application to confirm it bound to the correct port.
key_concepts.common_mistakes: Confusing LISTEN (waiting for connections) with ESTABLISHED (connected). A service must be in LISTEN state to accept new connections.
key_concepts.real_world_example: A DevOps engineer deploys a microservice and discovers it's listening on port 8081 instead of 8080. ss -lntp reveals the issue immediately — wrong port in the config file.
key_concepts.remember: LISTEN = service is ready to accept connections. If ss shows no LISTEN on your expected port, the service didn't start or is using a different port.
key_concepts.summary: ss -lntp shows listening ports. curl tests HTTP connectivity. These are your first tools when debugging 'why can't I reach my app?'

key_concepts.title: Background Processes and Job Control
key_concepts.definition: Linux lets you run processes in the background so your terminal stays available for other commands. Job control allows you to pause, resume, and manage these background tasks.
why_important: Run long-running scripts without tying up your terminal.
Continue working in the same terminal while a task runs in background.
Bring background jobs to foreground when you need to interact with them.
Keep processes running after you log out using nohup.
key_concepts.explanation:
Running processes in background:

```bash
command &          # Start in background
Ctrl+Z             # Pause (stop) foreground process
bg                 # Resume paused process in background
fg                 # Bring background process to foreground
jobs               # List all background jobs
```

Job management:

| Command | Action |
|:---|:---|
| `command &` | Start directly in background |
| `Ctrl+Z` | Suspend current foreground job |
| `bg %1` | Resume job 1 in background |
| `fg %1` | Bring job 1 to foreground |
| `jobs` | List all jobs with status |
| `nohup cmd &` | Run command that survives logout |

commands.name: command &
commands.purpose: Runs a command in the background, freeing the terminal for other use.
commands.parameters: &: the ampersand at the end sends the process to background.
commands.output_explanation: [1] is the job number. 5678 is the PID of the background process.
commands.when_to_use: Use for long-running tasks (builds, backups, log processing) that don't need terminal interaction.
commands.real_world_use: Start a database backup script in the background: ./backup.sh & — then continue other work.

commands.name: jobs
commands.purpose: Lists all background jobs and their current status.
commands.parameters: None required. -l shows PID in addition to job number.
commands.output_explanation: Job 1 is running in the background. Job 2 is stopped (suspended with Ctrl+Z).
commands.when_to_use: Use to check what background processes are running in your current terminal session.
commands.real_world_use: Check jobs after running multiple scripts in background to confirm they are still running.

key_concepts.pro_tip: Use `nohup ./script.sh > output.log 2>&1 &` to run a script that continues after you log out, with all output saved to a log file.
key_concepts.best_practices: Always redirect background process output to a log file so you can review it later: `./script.sh > script.log 2>&1 &`
key_concepts.common_mistakes: Running a process in background without redirecting output — it still prints to your terminal, mixing with other commands.
key_concepts.real_world_example: A DevOps engineer starts a long database migration in the background with nohup, logs out, then checks the log file the next morning to confirm it completed successfully.
key_concepts.remember: Background jobs only survive as long as your terminal session — unless you use nohup or a process manager like systemd or supervisor.
key_concepts.summary: Use & to start in background, jobs to list, fg/bg to move between foreground and background. Use nohup to survive logout.

interactive_examples.objective: List all running processes and find a specific one.
interactive_examples.scenario: Your web app is returning errors. You need to confirm the application process is actually running.
interactive_examples.command: ps aux | grep -v grep | grep bash
interactive_examples.explanation: ps aux lists all processes. The pipe feeds it to grep to filter for 'bash'. grep -v grep removes the grep command itself from results.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: A bash process is running under the 'student' user with PID 1234.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Run ps aux | grep nginx to verify nginx is running before checking its logs.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting grep -v grep — your grep command also shows up in ps output, creating confusion.
interactive_examples.tips: Use `pgrep -l processname` as a cleaner alternative: it shows only matching PIDs and names.

interactive_examples.objective: Find out which process is using a specific port.
interactive_examples.scenario: Your app fails to start with 'Address already in use'. Something else is already bound to port 8080.
interactive_examples.explanation: ss -lntp lists all listening TCP ports with process info. grep filters for port 8080 specifically.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: A Node.js process (PID 5678) is listening on port 8080. This is the conflict causing your app to fail.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Use ss -lntp | grep :PORT whenever you get 'port already in use' errors during service startup.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Looking at firewall rules first — usually the issue is a local process conflict, not a firewall.
interactive_examples.tips: Use `lsof -i :8080` as an alternative — it gives similar information in a different format.

interactive_examples.objective: Send a graceful stop signal to a process.
interactive_examples.scenario: A background script is running that you want to stop cleanly without losing any data it's currently writing.
interactive_examples.explanation: sleep 120 & starts a background process. $! holds the PID of the last background command. kill sends SIGTERM (graceful) to that PID.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: The sleep process was started, its PID captured, and then SIGTERM terminated it gracefully after 2 seconds.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Stop a running backup script gracefully so it can finish writing the current chunk before exiting.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Jumping straight to kill -9 without trying SIGTERM first — SIGKILL prevents the process from cleaning up.
interactive_examples.tips: Use `kill -0 PID` to check if a process is still running without sending a stop signal.

interactive_examples.objective: Test if a web server is responding.
interactive_examples.scenario: After deploying a web application, confirm it's serving HTTP responses before announcing the deployment is complete.
interactive_examples.explanation: -s: silent (no progress). -o /dev/null: discard response body. -w: write-out the HTTP status code. This is a health check pattern.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: HTTP 200 means the server responded successfully. Any 5xx code means the server is running but broken. Connection refused means it's not running at all.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: CI/CD pipelines use this curl pattern as a health check after deployment before switching traffic.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Testing the external hostname when the service isn't externally accessible yet — always test localhost first.
interactive_examples.tips: Exit code matters: `curl -f http://host/path` returns exit code 1 on 4xx/5xx, making it usable in scripts with set -e.

interactive_examples.objective: Run a script in the background and monitor it.
interactive_examples.scenario: You need to run a long-running task but keep your terminal available for other work.
interactive_examples.explanation: sleep 30 runs in the background (&). Output redirected to log file. jobs immediately shows the background job status.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: Job 1 is running in the background. The + means it's the most recent background job.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Start a database backup in the background: `./backup.sh > backup.log 2>&1 &` then continue other work.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Not redirecting output — background process prints to your terminal, mixing with your other commands.
interactive_examples.tips: Use `tail -f /tmp/task.log` to monitor the background job's output in real-time.

lessons.title: 1. List Processes (ps aux)
lessons.definition: ps shows a snapshot of currently running processes.
lessons.explanation: ps aux lists every process for all users with CPU and memory details.
lessons.instruction: List all running processes using ps aux.

lessons.title: 2. Find a Process by Name (ps + grep)
lessons.definition: Combining ps with grep filters results to find a specific process.
lessons.explanation: Pipe ps aux output through grep to search for a named process.
lessons.instruction: Find all bash shell processes using ps aux piped to grep.

lessons.title: 3. Check Listening Ports (ss -lntp)
lessons.definition: ss (socket statistics) shows network connections and listening ports.
lessons.explanation: -lntp flags show listening TCP ports with numeric ports and process names.
lessons.instruction: Check all listening TCP ports using ss.

lessons.title: 4. Test HTTP Connectivity (curl)
lessons.definition: curl is a tool for making HTTP/HTTPS requests from the command line.
lessons.explanation: curl URL fetches content from a URL. Add -I for headers only.
lessons.instruction: Use curl to fetch http://example.com and display the response.

lessons.title: 5. Run Process in Background
lessons.definition: The & operator sends a command to run as a background process.
lessons.explanation: Appending & to any command lets it run while your terminal stays free.

lessons.title: 6. List Background Jobs (jobs)
lessons.definition: The jobs command lists background and stopped processes in the current terminal session.
lessons.explanation: Shows job number, status (Running/Stopped), and the command.

lessons.title: 7. Kill a Process (kill + PID)
lessons.definition: kill sends a signal to a process. Default signal is SIGTERM (graceful stop).
lessons.explanation: Find the PID with ps or jobs, then kill [PID] to send SIGTERM.
lessons.instruction: Kill the sleep background process using its job number with kill %1.

lessons.title: 8. Check Network Connectivity (ping)
lessons.definition: ping sends ICMP packets to test if a host is reachable on the network.
lessons.explanation: ping hostname sends packets and reports round-trip time. Use -c to limit the count.
lessons.instruction: Ping localhost 3 times to test network stack.

exercises.title: Process Discovery
exercises.problem: Find the PID of your current bash session using ps and grep, then verify it with echo $$.
exercises.hint: ps aux | grep bash shows bash processes. $$ is a special variable holding your current shell's PID.
exercises.expected_result: Both ps grep output and echo $$ should show the same PID.
exercises.objective: Learn to identify and verify process IDs.

exercises.title: Port Audit
exercises.problem: List all TCP ports currently listening on this machine. How many are there? What services do you recognize?
exercises.hint: Use ss -lntp. Count the LISTEN lines. Common ports: 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 5432 (PostgreSQL).
exercises.expected_result: A list of listening ports with process names.
exercises.objective: Practice reading ss output and identifying common services.

exercises.title: HTTP Health Check
exercises.problem: Use curl to fetch the HTTP status code from https://httpbin.org/status/200. What code do you get? Now try https://httpbin.org/status/404.
exercises.hint: Use `curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" URL` to show only the HTTP status code.
exercises.objective: Learn the curl health check pattern used in CI/CD pipelines.

exercises.title: Background Job Manager
exercises.problem: Start three sleep commands in background (sleep 60, sleep 90, sleep 120). List all jobs. Then bring the first one to foreground and stop it with Ctrl+C.
exercises.hint: Run each with &, then jobs to see all three. fg %1 brings job 1 to foreground. Ctrl+C stops it.
exercises.expected_result: jobs shows [2] and [3] still Running after stopping [1].
exercises.objective: Practice job control: background, foreground, and stopping individual jobs.

exercises.title: Find the Process Eating CPU
exercises.problem: Run `yes > /dev/null &` to simulate a CPU-intensive process. Use ps aux or top to find its PID. Then gracefully kill it.
exercises.hint: yes > /dev/null & runs a CPU-intensive loop in background. ps aux | grep yes finds its PID. kill [PID] stops it.
exercises.expected_result: yes process stopped. CPU usage returns to normal.
exercises.objective: Simulate diagnosing and resolving a runaway process — a common SRE task.

exercises.title: Port Conflict Resolution
exercises.problem: Start a simple HTTP server on port 8000 (python3 -m http.server 8000 &). Now try to start another one on the same port — observe the error. Find the conflicting PID and kill it.
exercises.hint: First server: python3 -m http.server 8000 &. Second attempt shows 'Address already in use'. Use ss -lntp | grep 8000 to find the PID, then kill it.
exercises.expected_result: After killing the first server, second one starts on port 8000 successfully.
exercises.objective: Experience and resolve a real port conflict scenario.

exercises.title: Persistent Background Task
exercises.problem: Use nohup to run a script that writes the current date every 5 seconds to a file `heartbeat.log`. Verify it's running with jobs. Simulate logging out by killing the terminal (pkill -SIGHUP bash).
exercises.hint: Create a while loop script first. Then: `nohup ./heartbeat.sh > heartbeat.log 2>&1 &`. nohup makes it survive terminal close.
exercises.expected_result: heartbeat.log continues growing after the terminal session ends.
exercises.objective: Understand nohup and why daemons use it to survive terminal close.

exercises.title: Process Signal Investigation
exercises.problem: Start a sleep 300 & process. Send it SIGSTOP (kill -19 PID). Check ps and observe the 'T' (stopped) state. Then send SIGCONT (kill -18 PID) to resume it. Verify it's running again.
exercises.hint: sleep 300 & gives you the PID. kill -19 $PID stops it. ps aux | grep sleep shows state T. kill -18 $PID resumes.
exercises.expected_result: Process transitions from Running → Stopped (T) → Running again.
exercises.objective: Understand SIGSTOP and SIGCONT signals used in process control.

exercises.title: Complete Service Health Check Script
exercises.problem: Write a script `healthcheck.sh` that: checks if port 22 (SSH) is listening, pings localhost 3 times, and reports PASS or FAIL for each check.
exercises.hint: Use ss -lntp | grep :22 for port check. ping -c 3 localhost for connectivity. Use if/else based on exit codes.
exercises.expected_result: Script prints PASS/FAIL for each check when executed.
exercises.objective: Build a real-world health check script combining networking and process knowledge.

exercises.title: Process Tree Analysis
exercises.problem: Start a bash subshell (bash &) that starts its own sleep (bash -c 'sleep 300' &). Use ps f (forest view) to visualize the process hierarchy. Then kill the parent bash and observe what happens to the child sleep.
exercises.hint: ps f shows a tree view of processes. Killing the parent bash may orphan the child (which init adopts) or kill it depending on the setup.
exercises.expected_result: Understanding of parent-child process relationships and orphan processes.
exercises.objective: Understand process trees — critical knowledge for container and daemon management.

quiz.question: Which command shows all running processes with CPU and memory details?
quiz.explanation: ps aux shows all processes (a), user format (u), including those without a terminal (x). It's the standard command for a full process snapshot.

incorrect_explanations.ls -la: ls lists files and directories, not processes.
incorrect_explanations.top -all: top is valid but -all isn't a flag. The plain top command works.
incorrect_explanations.jobs -v: jobs only shows background jobs in the current terminal session, not all system processes.

quiz.question: What is the difference between SIGTERM and SIGKILL?
options: No difference — both kill the process immediately
SIGTERM asks the process to stop gracefully; SIGKILL forces immediate termination
SIGKILL is for terminals; SIGTERM is for background processes
quiz.answer: SIGTERM asks the process to stop gracefully; SIGKILL forces immediate termination
quiz.explanation: SIGTERM (15) lets the process save state and clean up. SIGKILL (9) is unblockable — the OS terminates the process immediately without cleanup.

incorrect_explanations.No difference — both kill the process im: SIGTERM can be caught by the application — it may delay or ignore it. SIGKILL cannot be caught.
incorrect_explanations.SIGKILL is for terminals; SIGTERM is for: Both work on any process. The difference is graceful vs forced.
incorrect_explanations.SIGTERM is stronger than SIGKILL: SIGKILL is stronger — SIGTERM can be ignored. SIGKILL cannot.

quiz.question: Which command checks which process is listening on a specific port?
quiz.explanation: ss -lntp (socket statistics, listening, numeric, TCP, processes) shows which processes are listening on which ports.

incorrect_explanations.ps -p PORT: ps -p takes a PID, not a port number.
incorrect_explanations.kill -PORT: kill sends signals — it doesn't inspect ports.
incorrect_explanations.jobs PORT: jobs lists background jobs in the current session — not network ports.

quiz.question: You run `sleep 60 &`. What does the output `[1] 5678` mean?
options: [1] is the PID, 5678 is the job number
[1] is the job number, 5678 is the PID
[1] is the exit code, 5678 is the process count
Both numbers are PIDs of two processes
quiz.answer: [1] is the job number, 5678 is the PID
quiz.explanation: [1] is the shell's job tracking number. 5678 is the actual OS Process ID. Use kill %1 (job) or kill 5678 (PID) to stop it.

incorrect_explanations.[1] is the PID, 5678 is the job number: This is reversed. Job numbers appear in square brackets.
incorrect_explanations.Both numbers are PIDs of two processes: Only one process is started. Two numbers appear: job number and PID.

quiz.question: Scenario: A web service is not responding. ss -lntp | grep :8080 returns no results. What does this tell you?
options: The service is running but blocked by a firewall
The service is not listening on port 8080 — it may have crashed or started on wrong port
Port 8080 is in use by another process
The ss command is not working correctly
quiz.answer: The service is not listening on port 8080 — it may have crashed or started on wrong port
quiz.explanation: No LISTEN on port 8080 means nothing is bound to that port. The service either crashed, failed to start, or is configured to use a different port.

incorrect_explanations.The service is running but blocked by a: A firewall blocks external access but ss still shows local listening sockets regardless of firewall rules.
incorrect_explanations.Port 8080 is in use by another process: If another process were using 8080, ss would show it — but ss shows nothing.
incorrect_explanations.The ss command is not working correctly: ss is a reliable tool — no results genuinely means no listener on that port.

quiz.question: Which command brings a background job (job number 2) to the foreground?
quiz.explanation: fg (foreground) brings a specified job to the foreground. %2 specifies job number 2.

incorrect_explanations.jobs %2: jobs only lists jobs — it doesn't move them.
incorrect_explanations.bg %2: bg (background) resumes a stopped job in the background — the opposite of fg.
incorrect_explanations.front %2: front is not a valid command.

quiz.question: What does `curl -I http://localhost` do?
options: Downloads the full page to a file
Sends only a HEAD request and returns HTTP headers
quiz.answer: Sends only a HEAD request and returns HTTP headers
quiz.explanation: -I flag sends an HTTP HEAD request — gets headers only without downloading the body. Useful for checking if a server is up and its status code.

incorrect_explanations.Downloads the full page to a file: -o filename downloads to a file. -I only fetches headers.
incorrect_explanations.Installs curl on localhost: curl is a transfer tool, not an installer.
incorrect_explanations.Pings localhost on port 80: ping uses ICMP, not HTTP. curl uses HTTP.

quiz.question: What signal number is SIGKILL?
quiz.explanation: SIGKILL is signal 9. It cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored by the receiving process — the OS always terminates it.

incorrect_explanations.1: Signal 1 is SIGHUP — used to reload configuration.
incorrect_explanations.15: Signal 15 is SIGTERM — the graceful termination signal.
incorrect_explanations.19: Signal 19 is SIGSTOP — pauses a process (like Ctrl+Z).

quiz.question: What does the `-c 4` flag do in `ping -c 4 google.com`?
options: Sends exactly 4 ICMP packets and then stops
Sets a connection timeout of 4 seconds
quiz.answer: Sends exactly 4 ICMP packets and then stops
quiz.explanation: -c COUNT limits the number of ping packets sent. Without -c, ping runs indefinitely until Ctrl+C.

incorrect_explanations.Connects with 4 parallel streams: -c is not related to parallelism in ping.

quiz.question: Scenario: You want to run a database backup script but keep using your terminal. Which command achieves this?
quiz.explanation: Appending & sends the process to the background, immediately returning terminal control to you.

incorrect_explanations../backup.sh: Without &, your terminal is tied up until the script finishes.
incorrect_explanations.bg ./backup.sh: bg resumes an already-stopped process. It doesn't start new background processes.
incorrect_explanations.stop ./backup.sh: stop is not a standard Linux command for this purpose.

quiz.question: What state does a process enter when you press Ctrl+Z?
quiz.explanation: Ctrl+Z sends SIGSTOP to the foreground process, suspending it. It shows as 'Stopped' in jobs output. Use fg to resume or bg to continue in background.

incorrect_explanations.Running in background: The process is suspended/paused, not running. Use bg after Ctrl+Z to run in background.
incorrect_explanations.Zombie: Zombie processes are children that exited but whose parent hasn't read their exit code.

quiz.question: You run `ps aux | grep myapp` and see the process. The second column shows 4567. What is 4567?
quiz.explanation: In ps aux output, the columns are: USER, PID, %CPU, %MEM, VSZ, RSS, TTY, STAT, START, TIME, COMMAND. PID is always the second column.

incorrect_explanations.The memory usage in KB: %MEM is the 4th column and is a percentage, not KB. VSZ (virtual size in KB) is the 5th column.
incorrect_explanations.The CPU usage percentage: %CPU is the 3rd column, not the second.
incorrect_explanations.The port number: ps aux does not show port numbers — use ss or netstat for port information.

resources.summary: Process management and networking are fundamental SRE and DevOps skills. ps/top monitor processes, kill/pkill stop them, ss shows open ports, curl tests HTTP, and job control (&, fg, bg) manages background tasks. These commands are used daily in production environments.

resources.cheat_sheet:
# Linux Networking & Process Management Cheat Sheet

## Process Management
ps aux               # List all processes
ps aux | grep NAME   # Find process by name
top                  # Real-time process viewer
htop                 # Interactive top (if installed)
pgrep NAME           # Get PID by process name
pkill NAME           # Kill process by name

## Killing Processes
kill PID             # Send SIGTERM (graceful)
kill -9 PID          # Send SIGKILL (force)
kill -15 PID         # Explicit SIGTERM
kill -1 PID          # Send SIGHUP (reload config)

## Signal Numbers
1  = SIGHUP   (reload)
2  = SIGINT   (Ctrl+C)
9  = SIGKILL  (force stop)
15 = SIGTERM  (graceful stop)
19 = SIGSTOP  (Ctrl+Z)
18 = SIGCONT  (resume)

## Network & Ports
ss -lntp             # List listening TCP ports
ss -lntp | grep :80  # Check specific port
lsof -i :8080        # Which process uses port
ping -c 4 host       # Test connectivity
curl URL             # HTTP request
curl -I URL          # Headers only (HEAD)
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" URL  # Status code only

## Background Jobs
command &            # Run in background
jobs                 # List background jobs
fg %1                # Bring job 1 to foreground
bg %1                # Resume job 1 in background
nohup cmd &          # Survive terminal logout
Ctrl+Z               # Suspend foreground process
Ctrl+C               # Terminate foreground process

commands_table.name: ps aux
commands_table.description: List all running processes with details.

commands_table.name: pgrep
commands_table.description: Get PID of process by name.

commands_table.name: ss
commands_table.description: Show listening TCP ports and processes.

commands_table.name: curl
commands_table.description: Make HTTP requests from command line.

commands_table.name: ping
commands_table.description: Test network connectivity with ICMP packets.

commands_table.name: jobs
commands_table.description: List background jobs in current session.

commands_table.name: nohup
commands_table.description: Run command that survives terminal logout.

revision_notes: ps aux gives a snapshot. top gives live updates. Use both for different scenarios.
Always try SIGTERM (15) before SIGKILL (9). Graceful first, force only when necessary.
LISTEN in ss output means the service is ready. No LISTEN means the service isn't running.
& sends to background. fg brings back. Ctrl+Z suspends. bg resumes in background.
nohup makes a process survive terminal close — essential for long-running tasks.
best_practices: Never start with kill -9. Try kill PID first and wait 5 seconds.
Always verify a service is listening on the expected port after deployment.
Redirect background process output to log files so you can review it later.
Use pgrep -l before killing to confirm you have the right process.
beginner_mistakes: Running kill -9 as the first response to a stuck process.
Confusing port LISTEN state with ESTABLISHED — a service must LISTEN to accept connections.
Not redirecting background job output — it prints to your terminal unexpectedly.
Grepping ps output and being confused by the grep process appearing in results.

interview_questions.question: How would you find which process is using port 8080 and kill it?
interview_questions.answer: ss -lntp | grep :8080 shows the process name and PID. Then kill [PID] sends SIGTERM. If it doesn't stop, kill -9 [PID] forces termination.

interview_questions.question: What is the difference between a zombie process and an orphan process?
interview_questions.answer: A zombie process has finished executing but its parent hasn't collected its exit status (wait()). An orphan process's parent has died — init (PID 1) adopts it automatically.

books.description: Excellent chapters on process management and job control with practical examples.
